complaint_details.detailed_description_of_your_complaint:
I am writing to make a formal complaint about the way our EAP4-INT-03 (Academic English 4 - Intermediate) class has been conducted this term, specifically regarding the organisation and delivery of the class and the conduct of our teacher, Mr. James Turner.

I want to be clear that my goal is not to get anyone in trouble personally, but to express my concerns so that the learning environment can improve, both for me and for other students in future terms. I have tried to resolve some of these issues informally by speaking with the teacher and with classmates, but the situation has not improved.

1. Irregular class schedule and frequent late starts
Since the beginning of the term (starting around 2025-10-01), our class is scheduled from 9:00 am to 12:00 pm, Monday to Thursday. However, on at least 7–8 different occasions, Mr. Turner arrived between 15 and 30 minutes late. For example:
- On 2025-10-07 (Tuesday), he arrived at 9:22 am and simply said, “Sorry, traffic,” and then started the lesson without acknowledging the lost time.
- On 2025-10-21 (Tuesday), he arrived at 9:18 am and asked us to “just review your vocabulary list” while he set up the computer. We ended up starting the actual lesson at almost 9:30 am.
- On 2025-11-04 (Tuesday) and 2025-11-06 (Thursday), he was late again (around 15 minutes each time).

On several of these days, we were left sitting in the classroom unsure if class had been cancelled. Some students, including myself, checked Canvas and email but there were no announcements. This made me feel anxious because I am on a student visa and I am very careful about my attendance. I also work part-time in the evenings and plan my day carefully around my study schedule, so the late starts and uncertainty have been stressful.

2. Inconsistent information about assessments
There have been multiple times when the information given about assessment tasks has changed or been unclear, which has affected my performance and confidence.

For example, for our major writing assessment (argumentative essay) originally scheduled for 2025-11-13, Mr. Turner first told us on 2025-11-03 that the essay would be 800–1,000 words and that we could bring in a one-page plan with bullet points. A few days later, on 2025-11-10, he said that the word count was actually 1,200 words and that we were not allowed to bring any notes except a dictionary. Some students had already prepared plans based on the first instructions.

I asked him after class on 2025-11-10 to confirm the requirements because I was confused. He responded quite casually, saying something like, “Don’t worry so much about the word count; just write as much as you can.” However, the rubric on Canvas clearly stated 1,000–1,200 words, and I was worried that writing only 800 words would lower my grade.

Another example is the speaking presentation. At first we were told we could choose any topic related to our future studies, and that we would present individually. Later, about one week before the presentation (around 2025-10-28), he changed it to pair presentations and said we had to choose from a list of topics he provided. My partner and I had already spent time preparing slides on a different topic. When I asked if we could still use our original topic, he said, “You’ll be fine, just adapt it,” but we had to re-do almost everything in a short time.

3. Lack of clear feedback and sometimes dismissive comments
I appreciate that teachers are busy, but I feel that the feedback we receive is often very minimal and sometimes dismissive. For instance, on my first writing assignment, I received a grade with only short comments like “watch grammar” and “expand ideas” without specific examples of what to improve. When I stayed after class on 2025-10-16 to ask for more detailed feedback, Mr. Turner looked at my paper quickly and said, “Your English is good enough, you just need more practice,” and then turned to speak to another student. I felt embarrassed because I had waited until other students left to ask privately.

On another occasion, during a speaking activity (around 2025-11-06), I mispronounced the word “culture” and a few classmates laughed. Mr. Turner smiled and repeated the word correctly but then said, “You guys really need to watch more Netflix if you want to sound natural.” I know he probably meant it as a joke, but I felt self-conscious and it made me less willing to participate in speaking activities. English is my second language and I am trying very hard. Comments like this, especially in front of the whole class, feel discouraging.

4. Use of mobile phone during class
I have also noticed that Mr. Turner often checks his phone during class, especially during individual or pair work. Sometimes he stands at the back of the class scrolling for several minutes. On 2025-11-19, during a reading activity, he sat at the teacher’s desk and was on his phone for almost 10 minutes. A few students looked at each other and whispered about it. It made me feel that he was not fully engaged with us. When I had a question about a word in the reading, I waited until he finished whatever he was doing on his phone before I approached him.

5. Impact on my learning and emotional wellbeing
All of these issues together have made me feel quite stressed and unsure about my progress in this course. I moved from Ho Chi Minh City to Melbourne in February this year, and I am living away from my family for the first time. I am paying for my studies with the help of my parents and some savings from my part-time job at a café in the CBD. Because of this, I take my studies very seriously and I expect the classes to be run in a professional and consistent way.

The frequent late starts mean we sometimes rush through important grammar or academic writing skills. The inconsistent instructions about assessments make me feel that I might fail not because I am not capable, but because I did not understand last-minute changes. The lack of detailed feedback and the occasional jokes about our English level make me feel less confident to ask questions or participate, especially in speaking tasks.

I have tried to deal with this directly. On 2025-10-23, I politely asked Mr. Turner if it would be possible to have clearer written instructions and more time to ask questions about assessments. He said, “I’ll upload something to Canvas,” but nothing new was uploaded. On 2025-11-18, another classmate (Yuki) asked in front of the class if he could please not change the assessment requirements so close to the due date. He laughed and said, “You guys will be fine, trust me,” but again, nothing changed.

6. What I am requesting
I am not asking for Mr. Turner to be punished, but I am asking for:
- More consistent and timely communication about assessment requirements (for example, putting everything clearly on Canvas and not changing it at the last minute).
- A reminder to start classes on time and to inform us through Canvas or email if there is any delay or change.
- Encouragement for teachers to provide more specific and constructive feedback, especially when students ask for help.
- Some guidance or training for teachers about making jokes related to students’ language level, as it can be sensitive and affect our confidence.

If possible, I would also like someone (for example, a course coordinator) to review this term’s class and maybe check if other students have similar concerns. I am happy to speak in person with a student advisor or coordinator if needed. I am making this complaint now because we are getting close to the end of the term and I am worried that these issues might affect my final results and my ability to move on to my bachelor program next year.

Thank you for taking the time to read this long explanation. I apologise if my tone sounds too emotional, but this course is very important for my future, and I want to make sure that both I and other students can learn in a more supportive and organised environment.
